deck-algo.front: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array?
deck-algo.back: O(log n) — each step halves the search space.

deck-algo.front: When does Quicksort degrade to O(n²)?
deck-algo.back: Poor pivot choices on already sorted or adversarial inputs; mitigated by random pivot or introsort.

deck-algo.front: What data structure backs a LRU cache?
deck-algo.back: Hash map for O(1) lookup + doubly linked list for O(1) eviction order (or ordered map in some languages).

deck-sd.front: State the CAP theorem in one line.
deck-sd.back: Under network partition, you cannot have both strong consistency and full availability; you must choose a tradeoff.

deck-sd.front: Name two reasons to add a cache in front of a database.
deck-sd.back: Lower read latency, reduce DB load, absorb traffic spikes (with cache invalidation/consistency tradeoffs).

deck-sd.front: What is idempotency for HTTP APIs?
deck-sd.back: Repeating the same request has the same effect as doing it once — critical for retries and payments.

deck-react.front: What problem does the Fiber architecture solve?
deck-react.back: Incremental rendering: pause, resume, and prioritize updates so the main thread stays responsive.

deck-react.front: Why should hook order not change between renders?
deck-react.back: React relies on call order to associate state with hooks; conditional hooks break that mapping.

deck-remediation.front: Revisit: Why does simple hash sharding hurt when cluster size changes?
deck-remediation.back: Changing N in hash(key) % N reshuffles most keys → massive data movement. Prefer consistent hashing or logical partitions.

deck-remediation.front: Revisit: When is a message queue better than synchronous RPC?
deck-remediation.back: When you need decoupling, buffering under load, and independent failure/retry semantics between producers and consumers.
It memoizes a computed value to skip recomputation when dependencies are unchanged — not side effects or network I/O.

deck-remediation.front: Revisit: Name one benefit of cache-aside with TTL for read-heavy workloads.
deck-remediation.back: It protects the database from thundering herds on hot keys while allowing stale reads bounded by TTL (eventual consistency).
